---
title: "Agentic AI on the Command Line"
---

# Agentic AI on the Command Line

So far we have been talking *to* an AI in a chat box. Now we let it act *on* a project — read files, write files, run code, look at the results, fix what's broken, commit when done. This is the move from *assistant* to *teammate*, and the difference is bigger than it sounds.

The vehicle is **Claude Code**, an agentic CLI that lives in a terminal and works inside a real project directory. Anything you'd hand to a junior collaborator — clean this CSV, run this regression, regenerate this graph in the journal style — you can hand to it. The tradeoff: now it has hands, and you are responsible for what it touches.

You should arrive at this chapter with a working terminal (Chapter 11) and Claude Code installed (Chapter 12). If not, go back. Do not skip those.

::: {.callout-warning}
## A real shift in trust
With chat AI you copy code in and out manually — every step crosses a human gate. With agentic AI, the AI can write a file or delete one without asking. Read Chapter 18 (Reproducible Research) before you give it free rein on a real project.
:::

{{< include ../../week04/index.qmd >}}

## Where we go next

Chapter 15 ([Advanced CLI Workflows](15-advanced-cli.qmd)) takes Claude Code beyond "clever assistant" — `CLAUDE.md` files, custom skills, git-aware execution, and headless / autonomous mode. Chapter 16 then walks you through a full data-to-PDF-report pipeline using the CPS earnings case study.

## AI and me — chapter reflection

1. Where did the agentic CLI **support** me in a way the chat model couldn't?
2. Where did it **fail** me, and was the failure recoverable from a checkpoint or commit?
3. How did it **extend** me — what would have been unthinkable to attempt without it?
